annotations.label: Class P (informal)

anchor.snippet: The general class of questions that some algorithm can answer in polynomial time

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes polynomial-time Turing-machine computation via `Turing.TM2ComputableInPolyTime` but not the language class P itself.

annotations.label: Class NP (informal)

anchor.snippet: The class of questions where an answer can be verified in polynomial time

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of polynomial-time verification or the class NP.

annotations.label: Generalized Sudoku

anchor.snippet: Consider the following yes/no problem: given an incomplete Sudoku grid of size

annotations.note: No Sudoku decision problem or its NP-completeness appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class P

anchor.snippet: the class P consists of all decision problems

annotations.note: Polynomial-time Turing-machine computation (`Turing.TM2ComputableInPolyTime`) exists, but the class P of decision problems is not defined.

annotations.label: Class NP

anchor.snippet: the class NP consists of all decision problems whose positive solutions are verifiable in polynomial time

annotations.note: The class NP and polynomial-time verifiability are not formalized.

annotations.label: P is contained in NP

anchor.snippet: Clearly, P ⊆ NP.

annotations.note: Without classes P and NP defined, the inclusion P ⊆ NP is not stated.

annotations.label: The P versus NP question

anchor.snippet: Is P equal to NP?

annotations.note: The P versus NP question is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: NP-complete

anchor.snippet: NP-complete problems are problems that any other NP problem is reducible to in polynomial time

annotations.note: NP-completeness is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: NP-hard

anchor.snippet: NP-hard problems are those at least as hard as NP problems

annotations.note: NP-hardness is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cook–Levin theorem (SAT is NP-complete)

anchor.snippet: the Boolean satisfiability problem is NP-complete by the Cook–Levin theorem

annotations.note: The Cook–Levin theorem and NP-completeness of SAT are not formalized.

annotations.label: NP-complete problem in P implies P = NP

anchor.snippet: If any NP-complete problem is in P, then it would follow that P = NP.

annotations.note: This collapse statement is absent since P, NP, and NP-completeness are undefined.

annotations.label: Trivial NP-complete problem

anchor.snippet: a trivial NP-complete problem can be formulated as follows

annotations.note: No NP-complete problem instances are formalized.

annotations.label: NP-completeness by reduction

anchor.snippet: proof by reduction provided a simpler way to show that many other problems are also NP-complete

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes computable many-one reductions (`ManyOneReducible`) but not polynomial-time reductions or NP-completeness.

annotations.label: EXPTIME

anchor.snippet: the class EXPTIME is the set of all decision problems that have exponential running time

annotations.note: The complexity class EXPTIME is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: EXPTIME-complete

anchor.snippet: A decision problem is EXPTIME-complete if it is in EXPTIME

annotations.note: EXPTIME-completeness is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P ≠ EXPTIME

anchor.snippet: Because it can be shown that P ≠ EXPTIME, these problems are outside P

annotations.note: The time hierarchy result P ≠ EXPTIME is not formalized.

annotations.label: Time hierarchy theorem

anchor.snippet: by the time hierarchy theorem

annotations.note: The time hierarchy theorem from computational complexity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chess on N×N board is EXPTIME-complete

anchor.snippet: Examples include finding a perfect strategy for chess positions on an N × N board

annotations.note: No EXPTIME-completeness of generalized chess is formalized.

annotations.label: Fischer–Rabin (Presburger arithmetic lower bound)

anchor.snippet: every algorithm that decides the truth of Presburger statements of length n has a runtime of at least

annotations.note: Presburger arithmetic is formalized as a first-order language (`FirstOrder.Language.presburger`), but its doubly-exponential runtime lower bound is not.

annotations.label: Undecidable problems

anchor.snippet: Even more difficult are the undecidable problems , such as the halting problem

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the halting problem is not computable in `ComputablePred.halting_problem`, the canonical undecidable problem.

annotations.label: Class #P

anchor.snippet: One such class, consisting of counting problems, is called #P

annotations.note: The counting class #P is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: #P-complete

anchor.snippet: Many of these problems are #P-complete , and hence among the hardest problems in #P

annotations.note: #P-completeness is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ladner's theorem

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: if P ≠ NP, then there exist problems in NP that are neither in P nor NP-complete

annotations.note: Ladner's theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: NP-intermediate

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: Such problems are called NP-intermediate problems.

annotations.note: NP-intermediate problems are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph isomorphism problem

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The graph isomorphism problem is the computational problem of determining whether two finite graphs are isomorphic

annotations.note: Graph isomorphism is defined (`SimpleGraph.Iso`, `≃g`), but the computational decision problem of testing it is not.

annotations.label: GI NP-complete collapses PH to second level

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: If graph isomorphism is NP-complete, the polynomial time hierarchy collapses to its second level.

annotations.note: The polynomial hierarchy and this collapse result are not formalized.

annotations.label: Babai's quasi-polynomial GI algorithm

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The best algorithm for this problem, due to László Babai , runs in quasi-polynomial time

annotations.note: Babai's quasi-polynomial isomorphism algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Integer factorization problem

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The integer factorization problem is the computational problem of determining the prime factorization of a given integer.

annotations.note: The prime factorization of a natural number is formalized (`Nat.factorization`), but not framed as a computational problem.

annotations.label: Class co-NP

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The integer factorization problem is in NP and in co-NP

annotations.note: The complexity class co-NP (complements of NP languages) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Factorization NP-complete collapses PH to first level

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: If the problem is NP-complete, the polynomial time hierarchy will collapse to its first level

annotations.note: This conditional polynomial-hierarchy collapse is not formalized.

annotations.label: General number field sieve

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The most efficient known algorithm for integer factorization is the general number field sieve

annotations.note: The general number field sieve is not formalized.

annotations.label: Shor's algorithm

anchor.section: Problems in NP not known to be in P or NP-complete
anchor.snippet: The best known quantum algorithm for this problem, Shor's algorithm , runs in polynomial time

annotations.note: Shor's algorithm and quantum computation are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cobham's thesis

anchor.snippet: an assumption known as Cobham's thesis

annotations.note: Cobham's thesis is an informal assumption with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Graph minor testing in O(n^2)

anchor.snippet: the problem of deciding whether a graph G contains H as a minor , where H is fixed, can be solved in a running time of O ( n 2 )

annotations.note: Neither a graph-minor relation nor the Robertson–Seymour O(n²) test is formalized.

annotations.label: Simplex algorithm

anchor.snippet: An example is the simplex algorithm in linear programming

annotations.note: The simplex algorithm for linear programming is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P = NP implies NP = co-NP and P = PH

anchor.section: Reasons to believe P ≠ NP or P = NP
anchor.snippet: the result P = NP would imply many other startling results that are currently believed to be false, such as NP = co-NP and P = PH

annotations.note: co-NP, the polynomial hierarchy, and these implications are not formalized.

annotations.label: DLIN and NLIN

anchor.snippet: one obtains the classes DLIN and NLIN

annotations.note: The linear-time classes DLIN and NLIN are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: DLIN ≠ NLIN

anchor.snippet: that DLIN ≠ NLIN.

annotations.note: The separation DLIN ≠ NLIN is not formalized.

annotations.label: P characterized by FO + least fixed point

anchor.snippet: all such languages in P are expressible in first-order logic with the addition of a suitable least fixed-point combinator

annotations.note: Mathlib has first-order logic (`ModelTheory`) but no Immerman–Vardi descriptive-complexity characterization of P.

annotations.label: Fagin's theorem (NP = existential SO logic)

anchor.snippet: NP is the set of languages expressible in existential second-order logic

annotations.note: Fagin's theorem and existential second-order characterization of NP are not formalized.

annotations.label: Polynomial hierarchy (PH)

anchor.snippet: The languages in the polynomial hierarchy , PH , correspond to all of second-order logic.

annotations.note: The polynomial hierarchy PH is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P = NP iff P = PH

anchor.snippet: since P = NP if and only if P = PH

annotations.note: The polynomial hierarchy and this equivalence are not formalized.

annotations.label: Levin's SUBSET-SUM algorithm

anchor.snippet: It correctly accepts the NP-complete language SUBSET-SUM

annotations.note: Levin's optimal SUBSET-SUM search algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Decision problem

anchor.snippet: A decision problem is a problem that takes as input some string w over an alphabet Σ, and outputs

annotations.note: A decision problem corresponds to a formal language, formalized as `Language α := Set (List α)`, though Mathlib does not frame it via Turing-machine input/output.

annotations.label: Class P (formal)

anchor.snippet: Formally, P is the set of languages that can be decided by a deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine.

annotations.note: Mathlib has polynomial-time Turing-machine computation (`Turing.TM2ComputableInPolyTime`) but does not bundle it into the language class P.

annotations.label: Deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine

anchor.snippet: a deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine is a deterministic Turing machine M that satisfies two conditions

annotations.note: `Turing.TM2ComputableInPolyTime` captures computation by a (deterministic) Turing machine within a polynomial time bound, over the `FinTM2` model.

annotations.label: Class NP (verifier)

anchor.snippet: Formally, NP is the set of languages with a finite alphabet and verifier that runs in polynomial time.

annotations.note: Polynomial-time verifiers and the class NP are not formalized.

annotations.label: NP via certificate relation

anchor.snippet: L ∈ NP if, and only if, there exists a binary relation

annotations.note: The certificate-relation characterization of NP is not formalized.

annotations.label: Verifier and certificate

anchor.snippet: A Turing machine that decides L R is called a verifier for L

annotations.note: The notions of verifier and certificate are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: COMPOSITE ∈ NP

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that COMPOSITE ∈ NP by verifying that it satisfies the above definition

annotations.note: Without the class NP, membership of COMPOSITE in NP is not stated.

annotations.label: COMPOSITE ∈ P (AKS primality test)

anchor.snippet: COMPOSITE also happens to be in P, a fact demonstrated by the invention of the AKS primality test

annotations.note: Primality is decidable in Mathlib, but the AKS polynomial-time test and membership of COMPOSITE in P are not formalized.

annotations.label: NP-complete (formal)

anchor.snippet: L is NP-complete if, and only if, the following two conditions are satisfied

annotations.note: The formal definition of NP-completeness is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: NP-completeness via reduction from NP-complete

anchor.snippet: if L ∈ NP, and there is another NP-complete problem that can be polynomial-time reduced to L , then L is NP-complete

annotations.note: Mathlib has transitive computable many-one reductions (`ManyOneReducible.trans`) but no polynomial-time reductions or NP-completeness.

annotations.label: R ≠ RE

anchor.snippet: These classes are not equal, because undecidable but verifiable problems do exist

annotations.note: Mathlib defines computable (`ComputablePred`) and r.e. (`REPred`) predicates and proves the halting problem is r.e. (`halting_problem_re`) but not computable (`halting_problem`), witnessing R ≠ RE without stating it as a class inequality.

annotations.label: VP vs VNP problem

anchor.snippet: A similar problem exists in the theory of algebraic complexity : VP vs. VNP problem.

annotations.note: The algebraic-complexity classes VP and VNP are not formalized.

annotations.label: FPT vs W[1]

anchor.snippet: FPT vs. W[1] is an analogous problem in parameterized complexity.

annotations.note: Parameterized-complexity classes FPT and W[1] are not formalized.
